evaluation.headline: Lin is disengaging fast — not from burnout, but from being kept on a leash with no real feedback.
summary_bullets: Lin deflected every question about the late nights, the standup, and the unwritten rules — four consecutive pivots away from the manager's concern and toward autonomy and feedback gaps. The pattern is the signal: Lin is not avoiding the conversation, Lin is redirecting it to what actually matters.

The manager's notes frame this as a sustainability problem; Lin's answers frame it as a competence-recognition problem — 'I don't want to seem incompetent,' 'PRs get approved quickly but I'm not sure if that means they're good.' These are not the same problem and require different responses.

Lin's stated want — 'shipped one feature without needing approval at every step' — sits in direct tension with the current setup where PRs are rubber-stamped and every step requires sign-off. The approval process is both too loose (no real feedback) and too tight (no autonomy). That contradiction is what's driving the disengagement.
evaluation.understanding_paragraph: Lin is not burning out — Lin is under-trusted and under-invested in, and has started to feel it. The late nights the manager flagged are not the story; the absence of meaningful code review and the approval bottleneck are. What the session revealed is that Lin's pride in fixing the production bug alone ('nobody asked me to') is the clearest window into what Lin actually needs: a problem worth owning, without a permission layer in the way.

axes.meaning: Weak signal — 'energy is fine, just sometimes bored' is not a wellbeing read, it's a redirect. Not actionable on its own.
The defining signal of this session — boredom, invisible feedback, and no real ownership are actively eroding investment. Act now.
A real pattern: unwritten team rules, unclear PR standards, and an approval process with no signal value. This needs direct intervention.
No signal — growth wasn't surfaced directly. The autonomy ask implies stagnation, but this axis needs a dedicated conversation.

evaluation.brutal_truth_employee: Lin said 'I don't want to seem incompetent' when asked about asking for help — that fear is shaping behavior more than any workload. Fixing a production bug alone, unprompted, and being proud of it is not a sign of thriving; it's a sign of someone proving themselves in a vacuum because the formal feedback channels have gone silent.
evaluation.brutal_truth_manager: When Lin said 'PRs get approved quickly but I'm not sure if that means they're good or just unreviewed,' that was the clearest ask in the entire session — and the conversation moved on to energy levels. That was the moment to name a specific reviewer and commit to a real review cadence. The late-nights concern the manager brought in is real, but it kept pulling focus away from what Lin was actually offering.

next_actions.action: Message Lin naming one specific person on the team who will give substantive PR reviews — not 'we'll improve review culture,' one name and one commitment.
Identify one feature currently in flight and explicitly hand Lin full ownership — no approval checkpoints until ship. Name it, scope it, and say it out loud.
Ask Lin directly: 'What would it look like to own a feature end-to-end — what would you need from me to make that real in the next month?'

watch_for: If Lin fixes another production issue alone and doesn't mention it until asked, the autonomy gap has widened — the workaround is becoming the norm.
If Lin's PRs are still getting same-day approvals with no comments two weeks from now, the feedback commitment didn't land and engagement will drop further.

focus_points.label: Where her work actually moved the needle this cycle.
focus_points.reason: Whether the models and analyses she shipped translated into decisions or outcomes — or stayed as artefacts. Senior Data Scientists get evaluated on leverage, not output volume, and this is easy to skip in a performance conversation.

focus_points.label: End-to-end follow-through on her biggest project.
focus_points.reason: How they're closing the loop — from analysis to stakeholder buy-in to outcome tracked. Seniors who are strong technically sometimes hand off findings and walk away; worth naming whether that's the pattern or not.

focus_points.label: How her work lands with the people who act on it.
focus_points.reason: What the relationship looks like with product, engineering, or whoever consumes her outputs. At Senior level, the gap between 'technically right' and 'actually used' is often a stakeholder trust gap, not a craft gap.

focus_points.label: What she wants to be doing more of in the next year.
focus_points.reason: If the conversation stays purely backward-looking, you lose the half that motivates her. Performance & feedback for a Senior IC is also the right moment to ask where she wants to stretch — Staff track, domain depth, or something else entirely.

preparation.coreIssue: This performance conversation with a Senior Data Scientist should focus on leverage, not just delivery. Without manager notes, the key risk is defaulting to output review — models shipped, analyses completed — rather than probing whether her work actually changed decisions. At senior level, the real evaluation is end-to-end ownership and stakeholder trust, and both can look fine on the surface while quietly being the limiting factor.
preparation.openingQuestion: What's the one piece of work this cycle you're most confident actually changed how someone made a decision?
listenFor: whether she can name a specific downstream decision or outcome her work influenced, not just a deliverable she completed
if they describe stakeholder relationships as collaborative and ongoing, or transactional — hand off the analysis, move on
whether her growth aspirations point toward Staff-track influence and ambiguity, or toward deeper technical craft — both are valid but need different support
avoid: do not let the conversation stay in output review mode — volume of work shipped is not the performance signal at senior level
do not skip the forward-looking half of the conversation; if you run out of time, cut backward review before cutting the growth discussion
preparation.goodOutcome: You and your direct report have agreed on one specific area — impact visibility, stakeholder follow-through, or growth direction — where she will take a concrete action before the next check-in, and you have named it together explicitly.
preparation.suggestedAction: Before the meeting ends, name the one competency gap or growth edge that surfaced and agree on a single observable step she will take in the next two weeks — check in on it at your next 1:1.

evaluation.headline: Sarah gave nine answers totalling under thirty words — the session itself is the signal, and 'the project' is the only thread to pull.
summary_bullets: Every answer was under five words, and the one moment of specificity — 'the project' — was immediately deflected with 'Yeah.' when followed up. Something about that project is the story, and we don't know what it is yet.

The pattern across the session is not shyness or brevity — it's consistent refusal across nine distinct prompts, including a direct meta-question about what was making the conversation hard. That's not a bad day; that's a wall.

The manager named the pattern plainly at turn seven and still got nothing — which means the usual re-prompting tools are spent. The next move is not another question in a 1:1 format; it's a different setting or a direct offer of support outside the meeting.
evaluation.understanding_paragraph: The session revealed almost nothing about Sarah's work, but revealed something important about her state: she is either unwilling or unable to engage in this format right now, and 'the project' is the only crack in that wall. We don't know whether this is distress, conflict, disillusionment, or something personal — but the uniform shutdown across nine questions rules out topic-specific discomfort. The one thing we didn't know before: she flagged the project unprompted, which means it matters to her even if she won't say how.

axes.meaning: Worth noting — 'I don't know' to a direct welfare check is not neutral; watch over the next few weeks.
A real pattern: nine non-answers in a performance conversation is the defining signal — act on it.
Weak signal, not actionable on its own — 'Not sure' to next steps may reflect the shutdown, not genuine confusion.
Weak signal, not actionable on its own — no growth data was surfaced; this axis is unread, not negative.

evaluation.brutal_truth_employee: Sarah said 'the project' when asked what was actually going on — that was the one unguarded moment in nine turns. She knows what the issue is. The refusal to name it in this format, after a direct and fair invitation, means she has decided this conversation is not the place to say it. That's a trust or safety signal, not a communication style.
evaluation.brutal_truth_manager: When Sarah said 'the project' at turn seven, the follow-up was 'What about the project — what's happening with it?' — a clean question that got 'Yeah.' The manager did everything right structurally. The one thing to deepen next time: after 'the project,' try naming a specific hypothesis out loud — 'Are you stuck on it, or is something wrong with how it's going?' — to give her a yes/no door instead of an open one she won't walk through.

next_actions.action: Send Sarah a short, low-pressure message: name that the 1:1 felt stuck, say you're not going to chase it in writing, and offer a 15-minute informal conversation this week — no agenda, no notes.
Find out independently what is happening with Sarah's project — talk to the product or engineering lead who owns the adjacent work and get a factual read before the next conversation with her.
Open with a specific hypothesis about the project — name what you've heard or observed — and ask her to confirm or correct it. Remove the blank-page prompt entirely.

watch_for: If Sarah responds to the informal outreach with another one-line deflection or doesn't respond at all, the disengagement is deeper than a bad meeting day — escalate to a direct welfare check.
If she mentions 'the project' again unprompted in any forum — Slack, standup, next 1:1 — that thread is still live for her and is the entry point; don't let it pass without pulling on it.

focus_points.label: Where his leverage is highest right now — and whether he feels it.
focus_points.reason: Whether the work he's leading is actually moving the product strategy, or whether he's drifted into execution and coordination. Senior Heads of Product get evaluated on the size of the bets they own, not the quality of their process.

focus_points.label: What the next step looks like and whether he's actively building toward it.
focus_points.reason: How they're thinking about the gap between where they are and where they want to go. Senior PMs at this level often have a clear ceiling in sight but no explicit plan — a Growth & career plan conversation is where that gets named.

focus_points.label: How he's building trust with the people above and beside him.
focus_points.reason: What his relationships with exec peers and cross-functional leads actually look like right now. At Senior Head of Product, the ceiling on career progression is almost always a stakeholder trust problem before it's a craft problem.

focus_points.label: Whether he's freeing himself up for the work only he can do.
focus_points.reason: If he's still hands-on in areas his team could own, he's not building the track record a Growth & career plan needs to point to. Worth asking what he's actively let go of in the last quarter.

preparation.coreIssue: This growth conversation with a Senior Head of Product should test whether he has a clear-eyed view of his own ceiling and a plan to move through it. Without manager notes, the risk is that he's performing well at the execution layer but hasn't explicitly named what the next level requires — or whether his current work is building toward it.
preparation.openingQuestion: What's the highest-leverage thing you're working on right now, and how much of your week is it actually getting?
listenFor: whether he distinguishes between owning strategic bets versus coordinating execution across teams
whether he can name a concrete next career step and at least one gap he's actively closing
if they reference specific exec or cross-functional relationships as assets — or avoid the topic entirely
avoid: do not offer a career framework or next-level criteria before he's articulated his own view — you'll anchor him to your model instead of surfacing his
do not conflate busyness with impact; a Senior Head of Product can be fully occupied and still be operating below their leverage point
preparation.goodOutcome: You and {{NAME}} have agreed on one specific area where he will shift time or ownership in the next four weeks to operate at higher leverage.
preparation.suggestedAction: Ask him to send you, within 48 hours, a one-paragraph answer to: 'What is the one bet I'm personally accountable for that would most change the product strategy if it succeeds?' — review it together at the next 1:1.

bank.name: Where in your current scope do you feel like you're having the most real influence on product direction — not just process?

evaluation.headline: Ahmed is a Senior Head of Product answering like a new engineer — the role mismatch is the story, not the codebase.
summary_bullets: The transcript is almost entirely about individual contributor concerns — codebase complexity, pairing, PR reviews, shipping a feature solo — with zero mention of strategy, stakeholders, or team leverage; this is a Senior Head of Product who is either operating far below level or describing a completely different job than the one he holds.

Ahmed deflects every forward-looking question into a tactical or interpersonal fragment: asked about 18 months from now, he talks about standup format; asked about unwritten rules costing him something, he pivots to a solo bug fix — the pattern of redirection is consistent and suggests he either can't or won't engage with the career-level questions.

The one genuine signal — 'I don't want to seem incompetent' — is the thread that connects everything: fear of exposure is likely what's keeping him in execution mode and away from the visibility, stakeholder trust, and delegation work that actually define his level.
evaluation.understanding_paragraph: Ahmed's answers reveal someone managing significant imposter anxiety at a senior level — 'I don't want to seem incompetent' is not a throwaway line, it's the operating principle behind every deflection in this session. The manager had no prior notes, so this is a first read: Ahmed is not coasting, he's hiding. The boredom he names ('sometimes bored by the ticket work') is likely a symptom of self-imposed scope restriction, not a lack of motivation.

axes.meaning: Weak signal — 'energy is fine, not burned out' is self-reported and unprobed; not actionable on its own.
A real pattern: boredom with ticket work plus no ownership signal at the right level — worth acting on now.
Marginal positive from a concrete six-month goal, but it's an IC goal from a Senior Head — the clarity is at the wrong altitude.
Weak negative — growth questions were consistently deflected; what we have is a partial read, not a verdict.

evaluation.brutal_truth_employee: Ahmed said 'I don't want to seem incompetent' in the second turn and then spent the rest of the session proving he's competent at the wrong things. A Senior Head of Product who measures himself by solo bug fixes and clean PRs has quietly contracted his own scope — and that contraction will become the ceiling if it isn't named directly.
evaluation.brutal_truth_manager: When Ahmed pivoted from the 18-month question to standup format, and from the unwritten-rules question to a bug fix he was proud of, the conversation moved on both times. Those pivots were the signal — Ahmed was showing exactly what he reaches for when the career-level question feels too exposed. The next session needs to hold the discomfort: name the pivot out loud and ask what he was moving away from.

next_actions.action: Meet with Ahmed for 20 minutes specifically to confirm his actual role scope — ask him to describe what he owns at the strategy and stakeholder level, not the execution level.
Set up one concrete stakeholder visibility moment for Ahmed — a review, a decision forum, or a cross-functional brief — so he has a real rep-building opportunity before next 1:1.
Open with: 'What's the biggest bet you're owning right now — not a feature, a strategic direction — and who else in the org knows you own it?'

watch_for: If Ahmed's answers in the next 1:1 are still framed around individual delivery — PRs, features, pairing — the scope contraction has become his default identity and needs a direct conversation, not another question.

If Ahmed proactively mentions a stakeholder interaction or a strategic decision he drove before the next 1:1, the imposter anxiety is manageable and he's capable of operating at level when given permission.

focus_points.label: Motivation and energy at the three-month mark.
focus_points.reason: Three months in is a common inflection point — the initial excitement has settled and the real texture of the job becomes clear. Worth asking directly how he's feeling about the work right now, without assuming the answer.

focus_points.reason: What the day-to-day actually feels like for a junior three months in — whether he feels like he belongs, whether he has people he can ask things without feeling like a burden. That gap is easy to miss from the outside.

focus_points.label: Whether the bar for good work feels clear to him.
focus_points.reason: Juniors at this stage often don't know what 'doing well' looks like from the manager's view. If something feels off, unclear expectations are one of the first places to check — not as a performance conversation, just as an honest calibration.

focus_points.label: What he'd want more of from you right now.
focus_points.reason: How he experiences your support three months in — whether he feels like he can come to you, or whether he's been quietly figuring things out alone. Juniors often don't ask until someone makes it easy.

preparation.coreIssue: Your junior product designer is three months in — past the honeymoon period and now living the real texture of the role. Something feels off, and at this seniority that often surfaces as quiet withdrawal rather than stated concern. The risk areas are role clarity, belonging, and whether he feels safe enough to ask for help — none of which he's likely to raise unprompted.
preparation.openingQuestion: What's felt hardest to figure out in the last few weeks — about the work, the team, or just how things work here?
listenFor: whether he can name specific people on the team he feels comfortable going to with questions
whether his description of 'good work' matches your actual bar, or reveals a gap in expectations
if they hedge or deflect when asked about support — that pattern often signals he's been quietly going it alone

preparation.goodOutcome: You and your direct report have agreed on one concrete thing you'll do differently to support him, and he can name what 'doing well' looks like in your eyes.
preparation.suggestedAction: Before the meeting ends, ask him to name one thing he's been unsure about but hasn't raised — then follow up on it specifically at your next 1:1 in two weeks.

evaluation.headline: Kenji is stuck, frustrated, and quietly carrying it — the 'something feels off' read is confirmed.
summary_bullets: Kenji's answers never tracked the questions asked — he answered energy questions with ambition statements, connection questions with shipping updates, and follow-ups with entirely new complaints. This isn't distraction; it's someone who came in with things to say and no safe opening to say them.

The frustration with changing requirements and the 'same kind of work for six months' signal land in the same place: Kenji feels like he's executing without influence, and the roadmap ownership ask is the direct expression of that.

The skipped follow-up on the API migration and the one-word 'Fine' to the energy thread suggest Kenji is not comfortable going deep on personal state — the real signal came only when the conversation drifted to structural complaints he could frame as work problems.
evaluation.understanding_paragraph: Kenji arrived with a specific frustration — lack of ownership and repetitive work — that he couldn't or wouldn't name directly when asked. The session confirmed the 'something feels off' flag, but the source is stagnation and powerlessness, not team fit or energy collapse. What's new: the roadmap ownership ask is concrete and actionable, not vague career noise.

axes.meaning: Quietly absorbing friction alone — 'more ownership over the roadmap would help' is a workaround request, not a wellbeing answer, and that's the tell.
Worth noting: still shipping and naming next steps, but 'stuck' and 'same kind of work' are early disengagement signals to watch over coming weeks.
Weak signal, not actionable on its own — one clear closing statement doesn't offset the scattered answers throughout.
No signal — growth history is empty, and the ambition statements were too disconnected from questions to score as real reads.

evaluation.brutal_truth_employee: Kenji said 'honestly I feel a bit stuck — same kind of work for six months now.' He's three months into this team, which means he was already stuck before he arrived. That's not an onboarding problem — that's a pattern he brought with him, and it will resurface unless the work changes.
evaluation.brutal_truth_manager: When Kenji said 'Fine' to the energy follow-up, the conversation moved on. That was the moment to stop and name the mismatch: 'You just told me you want to lead a team in a year — Fine isn't an answer to how your energy is.' The deflection was visible; it needed a direct call-out, not a re-prompt about leadership scope.

next_actions.action: Schedule a 30-minute conversation specifically about roadmap ownership — ask Kenji to name one area where he wants input, and define what 'more ownership' would concretely mean for the next sprint.
Identify one upcoming work item that is genuinely different in scope or type from what Kenji has been doing, and assign it to him with an explicit framing of why.
Open with 'Last time you said you felt stuck — what's changed, if anything?' and hold the silence long enough for a real answer before moving on.

watch_for: If Kenji raises the roadmap ownership ask again without prompting in the next 1:1, he's still carrying it — the conversation this week didn't land.

If he skips or gives one-word answers to direct personal-state questions again next session, he's decided this isn't a safe space for that — and the relationship needs rebuilding before the work problems can be addressed.

evaluation.headline: Nina is doing senior-plus work invisibly, and the session kept slipping away from the questions that matter most.
summary_bullets: Nina pivoted away from three consecutive follow-up questions — on struggling reports, on the communication feedback, on firefighting — each time surfacing a new, deeper concern instead of answering. The pivots themselves are the signal: she has more on her mind than any single thread captured.

Leadership visibility is the throughline she kept circling: the reliability work isn't seen, stakeholder management is a named gap, and the communication feedback 'landed hard.' These aren't separate issues — they're one career-risk story she hasn't fully articulated yet.

The manager's notes flagged late nights and pace; Nina never mentioned either. She named calendar fragmentation and firefighting as drains instead — the sustainability concern is real, but the cause the manager assumed may be wrong.
evaluation.understanding_paragraph: Nina is not running hot because she loves to ship fast — she's running hot because structural problems (no alerting, fragmented calendar, under-performing reports) are eating her capacity and nobody in leadership is noticing the operational load underneath the features. The session revealed that she's already thinking about a strategic-direction role, and she suspects her communication style and visibility with leadership are the blockers. What we didn't learn — because the conversation kept moving — is what specifically the communication feedback said and how much it's shaken her confidence.

A real pattern: spending more time coaching struggling reports than building is eroding the work she finds meaningful — act on it.
Worth noting: clear on her immediate next step with Alex, but her broader role and what leadership expects of her at this level remains murky.
A real pattern: leadership doesn't see the operational work, and Nina knows it — her path to the strategic role she wants is currently invisible to the people who decide it.

What's actually been driving the late nights — is this the scope of the work, or something else pulling on your time?
Does the current pace feel like something you've chosen, or something you're managing around?
What are you currently carrying that isn't on anyone's radar but yours?
What are you actually committed to shipping in the next two weeks, and what's been asked of you that you've pushed back on?
Of everything in flight right now, what feels like it has real momentum and what feels like it's quietly stalled?
Is the work you're doing right now the kind of work you want to be doing at this point in your career?
When did you last have a day that felt genuinely light?
What would make the next two weeks feel meaningfully different from the last three?

evaluation.headline: Nina is carrying the team's operational weight invisibly, and the firefighting — not the late nights — is what's actually draining her.
summary_bullets: Nina deflects every follow-up question with a new, unrelated topic — struggling reports, communication feedback, stakeholder management, calendar chaos — suggesting she's either processing several things at once or avoiding the one that's hardest to name.

The visibility gap she names ('they see the features but not the reliability') is the thread connecting her strategic ambition, her managing-up gap, and her drain — but the conversation never stayed on it long enough to act on.
She committed to a difficult conversation with Alex about PR quality, which is the only concrete, self-directed action in the session — everything else was complaint or aspiration without an owner.
evaluation.understanding_paragraph: Nina's late nights aren't the real story — the firefighting is. She said 'what's draining me is the constant firefighting' unprompted, which is a harder admission than brushing off pace concerns. The session also revealed a strategic ambition (less hands-on, more direction-setting) that she's holding alongside a recognition problem she hasn't solved — and those two things are connected in a way she hasn't named yet.

axes.meaning: A real pattern: firefighting drain is named directly and unprompted — act on the alerting gap, not just the hours.
Worth noting: more time coaching than building, and heavy lifting without delegation — the work isn't feeding her right now.
Weak signal, not actionable on its own — one concrete next step (Alex conversation) partially offsets the noise.
Worth noting: leadership doesn't see the reliability work, and the path to the strategic role she wants is blocked by that invisibility.

evaluation.brutal_truth_employee: Nina said 'what's draining me is the constant firefighting' and then immediately pivoted to stakeholder management — she named the real cost and then moved away from it. The pattern across this session is deflection through topic-switching. She's carrying more than she's letting land in any single conversation.
evaluation.brutal_truth_manager: When Nina said 'I want to move into a more strategic role' in response to a question about her struggling reports, that was a significant pivot — and the conversation followed her into the new topic instead of holding the thread. That moment ('less hands-on, more setting direction for the org') was the clearest signal in the session; next time, name the redirect and ask her to hold both.

next_actions.action: Ask Nina directly: 'What would it take to get one alerting gap fixed in the next two weeks — and who owns it if not you?'
Send Nina one concrete example of how to frame reliability work for leadership — a short write-up format or a standing agenda item in the next leadership review.
Open with 'Did the Alex conversation happen, and what did you learn?' — hold the one commitment she made before accepting new topics.

watch_for: If Nina raises the alerting gap again without a fix in progress, the firefighting has become normalized — she's stopped expecting it to change.
If she doesn't mention the strategic role again in the next two sessions, she's shelved the ambition rather than pursuing it — that silence is disengagement, not resolution.

focus_points.label: Craft and polish — where Carlos is landing right now.
focus_points.reason: What the work actually looks like at this stage of his ramp is the core of a junior Performance & feedback conversation. Brand design at junior level lives or dies on taste and attention to detail — this is the right place to be concrete.

focus_points.label: Follow-through from brief to final delivery.
focus_points.reason: How they're closing the loop on work — without being chased — is one of the clearest differentiators between a junior who's growing and one who's plateauing. Worth naming what good looks like here.

focus_points.label: Feedback Carlos is getting — and whether it's landing.
focus_points.reason: Whether the direction and critique he's receiving in reviews is actually shaping his next attempt, or bouncing off. Juniors sometimes nod and don't absorb — and sometimes absorb but never hear they're on the right track.

focus_points.label: Where he wants his brand craft to stretch next.
focus_points.reason: If this is a Performance & feedback conversation, it should end with a direction, not just a score. What Carlos is hungry to get better at shapes what you assign him and how you frame the next cycle.

preparation.coreIssue: This is a performance and feedback conversation with Carlos, a junior brand designer early in his ramp. With no manager notes to draw from, the conversation should test two things: whether his craft and follow-through are meeting the bar for his level, and whether the feedback he's been receiving in reviews is actually changing his work. The risk at junior level is that both feel fine on the surface until they aren't.
preparation.openingQuestion: What's a piece of work from the last few weeks you'd do differently now — and what would you change?
listenFor: whether he can name specific craft decisions he's made or reconsidered, rather than speaking only in general terms
if they reference feedback from reviews unprompted, and whether they describe it as useful or just something to act on
whether he can articulate what he wants to get better at, or whether growth feels like something that happens to him
avoid: do not lead with your own assessment of his work before hearing how he reads it — you'll lose the diagnostic value of his self-awareness
do not let the conversation stay at the level of tasks completed; push for the quality and ownership layer even if it feels uncomfortable
preparation.goodOutcome: You and Carlos have agreed on one specific area of craft or ownership to focus on before your next 1:1, with a concrete piece of work to use as the test.
preparation.suggestedAction: Name the one growth focus you aligned on and assign Carlos a brief or task in the next week that directly exercises it — check in on it at your next 1:1.

bank.name: How has the last few weeks felt from your side — not the output, but the actual experience of doing the work?
Walk me through a piece of work from the last quarter where the craft decisions were actually yours — what did you decide and why?
Think of a brief you received recently — what did the path from that brief to final delivery actually look like, and where did it get bumpy?
When you get a critique in a review, what does your next attempt actually look like — what changes and what doesn't?
Where in your work do you think the gap between what you handed over and what it could have been is biggest?
When work stalls or sits without moving forward, what's usually behind that for you?
Is the feedback you're getting in reviews actually useful to you, or does it leave you unsure what to do next?
What's the area of brand craft you most want to get sharper at in the next cycle — and what's stopping you from pushing there already?
Given what we've talked about today, what's one specific thing you're going to do differently before we next meet — and when will you do it?

evaluation.headline: Carlos is disengaging quietly — flat growth, ignored OKRs, and a performance conversation that never got to performance.
summary_bullets: Carlos deflected every question about his own work — 'the team is in a better spot' instead of naming a piece of work he's proud of — suggesting he either can't point to standout output or doesn't want to.

He named growth stagnation and broken OKRs unprompted, but both skips landed on the follow-up questions that would have made those signals actionable — the session closed without knowing who's ignoring the OKRs or what 'being pushed' would actually require.
The stated commitment ('have the roadmap conversation with product') is a meeting, not a change — Carlos is pointing at a structural fix rather than owning a personal next step.
evaluation.understanding_paragraph: Carlos carries a clear frustration about being under-challenged, but he expresses it sideways — through systems ('OKRs get ignored') and teams ('the team is better') rather than through his own work. The writing gap he surfaced ('I think well but don't communicate it') was the one moment of genuine self-disclosure, and it was skipped. What we don't yet know is whether his flatness reflects a lack of investment in the work, a lack of confidence naming it, or both.

axes.meaning: Worth noting: context switching is a friction point, but one answer is thin — watch over the next few weeks.
A real pattern: flat affect from the opening, explicit stagnation signal, and OKRs framed as meaningless — act on this.
No signal either way — the session didn't reach priorities or role expectations, so this is an open question.
Worth noting: Carlos named the plateau himself, but the follow-up that would define what 'being pushed' means was skipped.

evaluation.brutal_truth_employee: Carlos said 'no one is really pushing me' — but when asked what pushing would look like, he pointed at OKRs, not at himself. That's a deflection. The writing gap he named ('I think well but don't communicate it') is the only moment he owned something personal, and he skipped the follow-up. Until he can name what he wants to be stretched on, the stagnation is partly his.
evaluation.brutal_truth_manager: When Carlos said 'the team is in a better spot' instead of naming his own work, the conversation moved on. That was the moment to say 'I'm asking about you specifically — what did you ship that you'd put your name on?' The performance conversation never actually reached Carlos's craft, polish, or ownership — the three things this meeting was designed to cover. Next time, hold the redirect until he answers the question asked.

next_actions.action: Ask Carlos directly: 'Name one piece of work from the last quarter you'd put your name on — one asset, one deliverable.' Get a specific answer before the next 1:1.
Find out who owns the quarterly OKR process and whether Carlos's OKRs are actually being reviewed — if they're not, that's a manager fix, not a Carlos fix.
Return to the writing gap: 'You said you think well but don't communicate it — show me one doc or spec where that gap cost you, and let's fix it together.'

watch_for: If Carlos shows up to the roadmap conversation with product and reports back with a concrete outcome, his engagement is recoverable — if he doesn't schedule it or deprioritizes it, the disengagement is deeper than frustration.

If he deflects the 'name your own work' question again next session with a team or process answer, he's either avoiding accountability or genuinely doesn't know what good looks like for himself — both need a different intervention.
